channel.title: Radio National Breakfast - Breakfast Wrap
channel.description: Filtered feed containing only Breakfast Wrap episodes from Radio National Breakfast
channel.itunes:author: ABC Radio National

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: PM under fire over DV royal commission comments
item.description:
The Minister for Social Services, Tanya Plibersek has defended the Prime Minister amid criticism of his comments on calls for a Royal Commission into domestic violence. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Working on the Ebola frontline
item.description:
As Ebola infection numbers climb in central Africa, we hear from an infectious diseases physician who's previously worked on the world's biggest ebola outbreak more than a decade ago. 

Then, we turn to a separate health matter in Australia, as the federal government announces millions for diphtheria support package.

And we continue asking what questions people have about the government's tax changes. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Why this sector is alarmed by the tax changes
item.description:
Start-up founders are calling on the federal government to rethink ending the 50% capital gains tax discount - over fears it will cripple the sector.

The newly proposed inflation indexation model - announced in the federal budget last week - has been called the most punitive tax treatment on start-up equity in the western world by some in the sector.

We hear from all sides on the debate, including the federal government and an independent long calling for tax reform. 

Then, we get the latest on the Diphtheria outbreak from South Australia. 

And we cross to north London as Arsenal take out the EPL. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Why this retailer is ditching their tobacco license
item.description:
While public health advocates and the tobacco industry argue over the merits of lowering the tobacco excise, small businesses are caught in the middle. We hear from one who says he won't renew his tobacco license.

Then we get an update on the growing Ebola outbreak in the DRC and Uganda. 

And we speak with a Liberal concerned about his party's stance on immigration. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Ebola outbreak declared global health emergency
item.description:
The World Health Organisation has declared an Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of the Congo a public health emergency of international concern.

There have been around 250 suspected cases - and 80 deaths - reported in Congo's eastern Ituri province.

We hear from Professor Jane Halton, former Australian Health Department Secretary and current boss of the non-profit Coalition for Epidemic Preparedness Innovations.

Then, how different are the Coalition and One Nation on immigration policy? Shadow minister Dave Sharma gives his take. 

And NSW is undertaking an inquiry into the live music sector, so what is the message from festival organisers? 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Unpacking a big week in politics
item.description:
Can you believe the Farrer by-election was last weekend?

Its been such a big week in federal politics, with One Nation's win, the budget and the opposition's budget reply - we'll unpack all of it with our Friday political panel. 

We'll also hear from the former consumer watchdog after the federal court found Coles had broken the law by misleading consumers. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Trump and Xi's high stakes meeting
item.description:
Two of the world's most powerful leaders are about to hold a high-stakes meeting in Beijing.

We'll find out what's on the agenda and what both sides want from the discussions from a former advisor to the US National Security Council and the CIA.

Then, what questions do you have about the government's tax changes in the budget? We'll put them to a chartered accountant. 

And we'll speak with someone on the frontline of Ukraine's veteran mental health battle, as Russia's invasion continues for a fourth year. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Shorten vindicated by budget tax reforms
item.description:
Former Labor Opposition leader Bill Shorten says he's vindicated by negative gearing changes introduced by the Treasurer in last night's budget.

Mr Shorten attempted to bring in similar tax reforms in the 2019 election campaign that he ultimately lost to Scott Morrison.

Mr Shorten shares his views first with Radio National Breakfast. 

Then, we get the latest analysis on the budget from Tom Crowley, and hear from the Treasurer and Shadow Treasurer.

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Inside the budget lockup
item.description:
Its budget day! We hear from Carrington Clarke as he prepares for the lock-up in Parliament House. 

Finance Minister Katy Gallagher previews what we can expect, and her shadow across the aisle Claire Chandler lays out the Coalition's questions. 

Then we hear about the pressure British PM  Keir Starmer is under as multiple Labour MPs and Cabinet Ministers call time on his leadership.

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Albanese blames Coalition for One Nation's Farrer success
item.description:
PM Anthony Albanese says the "Liberal and National parties made a big mistake legitimising One Nation", following the weekend's Farrer by-election.

We hear from Mr Albanese, and Nationals defector turned One Nation MP Barnaby Joyce following the party's historic win. 

Then, a similar challenge to the major parties in the UK sees Keir Starmer's prime ministership under threat. 

And we'll get the latest on the US war with Iran. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Sir David Attenborough turns 100
item.description:
Today the beloved English broadcaster and natural historian David Attenborough celebrates 100 years on this earth and a lifetime of achievements.

We hear from one of his closest collaborators. 

We also follow developments in Iran with a former US diplomat, and get the latest on the Islamic State families who've now returned to Australia.

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Rolling news pioneer Ted Turner dies aged 87
item.description:
Ted Turner, who founded CNN in 1980 has died at the age of 87. 

Known for pioneering rolling 24-hour news coverage, he reshaped how audiences around the world consume news media. 

We hear from one of the people there at the birth of CNN, the first Australian CNN anchor Peter Ford. 

Then, what's the latest on the 13 Australians with ties to former Islamic State fighters due to arrive in Australia today? We'll hear from the Greens. 

And the Federal Government answers our questions as it tries to strike a major pact with Fiji. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: The seafarers stuck in the Strait of Hormuz
item.description:
Whatever is going on in the Strait of Hormuz, tens of thousands of seafarers remain trapped, unable to transit the waterway. 

We hear from one Indian seafarer who's been stuck for three months and the union trying to help him.

Then, as she delivered the third interest rate rise of the year, the RBA governor said this in response to a question,

'The extent to which government makes up the shortfalls for households by giving them more money, it makes it harder to dampen demand'.

So what is the government's response to the point that anything it does to support households makes the RBA's job more difficult? We ask the Federal Treasurer Jim Chalmers. 

And we hear about the government's latest attempt to bolster counter-terrorism efforts. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Why big tobacco was given a secret Senate hearing
item.description:
The federal government and the Greens are demanding answers after a Liberal-led Senate inquiry allowed tobacco giant Philip Morris to deliver evidence behind closed doors yesterday.

Liberal Senator Leah Blyth chairs the committee looking into illegal tobacco in Australia and granted the closed hearing - to the dismay of several health organisations already giving evidence.

We hear from one of the organisation's who gave its evidence publicly. 

Then, what is going on in the Strait of Hormuz? A former US Naval commander shares their insights. 

And we ask the government about gambling reform. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: How Australia conquered curling
item.description:
Is this Australia's Cool Runnings moment?

Australia's mixed team curlers Tahli Gill and Dean Hewitt have claimed world championship gold in Switzerland.

We hear from Dean fresh off the win. 

Then, the Japanese Prime Minister is in town for annual talks, so why is this a particularly important trip from the new leader?

And we'll return to Alice Springs days after the devastation of the death of a five year-old girl. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Grief and anger in Alice Springs
item.description:
As Alice Springs grieves the death of a five-year-old girl missing since the weekend, violence erupts outside the hospital where the accused killer is being held.

We hear from community organisations and police working in town as emotions boil over, and the alleged murderer is moved to Darwin for safety reasons. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Betting on a war
item.description:
What do you know about Polymarket?

We look at the platform letting people put a bet on basically anything - from sport to war. And how its being used to predict attacks in the Middle East. 

Then, ahead of next month's federal budget we hear from Shadow Treasurer Tim Wilson. 

And the former Israeli Prime Minister Ehud Olmert discusses the ongoing strikes in Lebanon.

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: The King's speech
item.description:
Overnight, King Charles followed in his mother's foot steps - telling the joint session of Congress that the bond between the US and UK is irreplaceable and unbreakable.

We'll unpack the historic address and look at how the royal visit is going. 

Then, Prime Minister Anthony Albanese has unveiled the government's long-awaited proposal to make tech giants pay for Australian journalism. 

And with the Foreign Minister in China, calls are growing for renewed attention on the ethnic minority Uyghur population.

 Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: 30 years since Port Arthur
item.description:
The Port Arthur massacre is remembered not just for the horror of that day, but for the political and societal response that followed, changing Australia forever.

Hear our interview with then Prime Minister John Howard, who played a major role in reforming the country's gun laws in the wake of the attack. 

We'll also cross to the US to hear from a former FBI agent following the White House Correspondents' Dinner shooting, and find out about a tech titan's court battle for the ages. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Inside the White House Correspondents' Dinner
item.description:
Lax security and entry tickets left in bathrooms. We hear what it was like inside the White House Correspondents' Dinner as a suspected gunman tries to cause chaos at one of the biggest events in Washington. 

Then, how will the attack impact the upcoming royal visit to DC? 

We'll also hear about the widely condemned boos at ANZAC Day ceremonies around the country, from someone who was targeted. 

And what is the impact of the government's major NDIS reform on those who use the services?

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Is the government's gas tax dead?
item.description:
The prime minister is poised to kill off a move to increase taxes on gas giants - echoing the industry's argument that exporters are already paying tens of billions of dollars in taxes each year.

You'll hear from a range of key voices in the gas tax debate.

The United Nations is confident it can get fertiliser shipments through the Strait of Hormuz within seven days, and save millions of people from food insecurity. We speak to the UN boss in charge of the mission.

Back home, fallout from the NDIS reforms continue, as the autism community fears many will be left without support when the federal government cuts 160,000 people from the scheme.

And the new director of Adelaide Writer's Week joins the show.

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: What do NDIS families think of these major reforms?
item.description:
The federal government has announced sweeping changes to the National Disability Insurance Scheme, in a bid to bring spending back down to sustainable levels, and clamp down on fraud.

On the program we bring you the reactions of participants and their families, as well as advocates and providers.

You'll also hear from one of the original architects of the NDIS, and Health Minister Mark Butler.

Meanwhile in the Strait of Hormuz, Iran has attacked three vessels, seizing two, as tensions rise in the vital waterway.

It comes as US President Donald Trump claims a ceasefire with Iran has been extended - but without a new date.

We'll unpack the state of the Strait.

 Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Major reforms ahead for NDIS
item.description:
Big changes are coming to the National Disability Insurance Scheme today - with Health Minister Mark Butler expected to announce measures to curb the growing cost of the scheme later today.

We dive into this with one of the original architects of the scheme, and Greens NDIS Minister, Jordon Steele-John.

In the Middle East, US President Donald Trump has announced a ceasefire between the US and Iran, hours before it was set to expire - you'll get the latest on a rapidly evolving situation.

The E-Safety Commissioner will also weigh in on her decision to put online gaming giants on notice, over fears that extremists and sexual predators are using these platforms to radicalise young people.

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.
